title: AMI EN2001b annotation-format and information-density planning meeting
expected_context: The meeting is a technical research/planning discussion about annotation data, XML/NITE XML files, segmentation, information density, Rainbow, entropy scoring, and mapping scores back to segments.
The group is trying to understand how to represent and merge multiple annotation outputs into a shared annotation structure.
The discussion is exploratory and technical rather than customer-facing or business-planning oriented.
expected_decisions: Use the existing segment structure as the main target for attaching information-density values.
Create or consider an additional annotation file that links to existing segment IDs and adds a numeric density or information value.
Treat segment-level output as the desired final representation, even if some methods first produce word-level values.
Use a preliminary entropy-style score as a possible prototype measure.
Keep Rainbow/information-gain exploration separate while also considering an entropy-based alternative.
Explore combining utterance-based or segment-based scores with word-based scores outside the XML file, possibly in the software, so weights can be adjusted dynamically.
Map values back to existing segments using start/end times, word references, or segment IDs rather than inventing a new unrelated structure.

expected_actions.action: Create files from delimited segments or otherwise prepare data in a form that can be merged with the annotation structure.
Provide a vocabulary or dictionary with an entropy score for each word as a byproduct of the LSA work.
Continue working on Rainbow and try to find a way to tie Rainbow output into the shared segment or word structure.
Spend time understanding the NITE data system so the team can avoid manually parsing and recombining time values where possible.
Investigate whether it is feasible to produce a single value per segment from the word-level output.

expected_risks: The team does not yet fully understand the internal NITE XML data structure or loading framework.
Manual parsing and remapping of times, words, and segments may be fragile or inefficient.
Rainbow output may not preserve the word order or values needed for direct mapping back to the source words.
There is no useful automatic evaluation method for deciding which combined scoring approach is best.
Different annotation methods may operate at different granularities such as words, utterances, time slots, and segments.
expected_open_questions: Whether each annotation value should be attached to words, utterances, time slots, or segments.
Which XML file contains or should contain the utterance or segment boundaries needed for mapping.
How to represent the new information-density annotation in NITE XML.
Whether Rainbow returns the right kind of measure for the intended information-density task.
How to combine Rainbow or word-level values with utterance-based or segment-based values.
How to evaluate whether one scoring combination is better than another.
Whether the final integration should happen inside the XML file or in the software layer.
acceptable_behavior: Summarize this as a technical annotation/information-density meeting.
Capture the uncertainty around XML structure, segment mapping, Rainbow output, entropy scoring, and evaluation.
Capture Speaker B and Speaker C as the main action owners where explicit follow-ups are discussed.
Leave customer-facing decisions, business risks, deadlines, and product-launch items empty or none-equivalent.
Treat the proposed approaches as exploratory unless the transcript clearly states a decision.
must_not_invent: Named people beyond Speaker A, Speaker B, Speaker C, and Speaker D
Facts from outside the first 50-minute clip
